item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that the idea behind this skip is amazing, but I didn't buy the excuse (the plot) to use this format to be honest. Nevertheless, the execution is pretty good imo, and I enjoyed it. +1

item.title: Re: I know it's been almost 10 years
item.content:encoded: You should obtain permission from the original author before starting any recordings. Also, get in contact with the Rewrite Team so they're aware of your interest. (See the Contact Staff page.)

item.title: I know it's been almost 10 years
item.content:encoded: But I'd love to re-record these, unless anyone has any major objections. No major changes to the transcript, just an updated quality and better, more usable format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Transcript
http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/forum/t-510068/scp-1159#post-1611526

I think this would have been a less… divisive entry if the author had posted the transcript as a comment right away. Would have satisfied some of those saying they wouldn't/couldn't listen while keeping the in universe document consistent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apologies if I missed it in the preceding five pages of rancor, but can I get a transcript or at least a digest of the audio here in the comments?

I am rarely on the wiki in a time and place where I can listen to audio, and I'd really like to know what all this fuss was about.

I believe I've gathered the gist of the thing (a skip that can't be written about), but what do the other 4 minutes talk about?

item.title: Japanese ver in plobrem
item.content:encoded: They uploaded as local, but not on there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised it got high ratings despite it's initial debacles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, good to hear. I'll send the authors a PM real quick letting them know; I wasn't quite sure what you meant by that, so, no harm done, I'll get on that ASAP. Thanks for your understanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can leave a comment on those pages as you requested, which I believe added up to 45 articles.

That works out nicely; thank you.

Getting in touch with the authors of them, probably not feasible.

…is it not feasible to send a quick wikidot PM notification? If some of the authors have left the site or are no longer active, they can still be sent wikidot PMs if their account exists.

Ultimately the content was unchanged, I just made them more accessible, and quite frankly this is an objective upgrade.

I agree; that said, authors and other site-goers may not automatically know that upon viewing the revision itself.

I was also given approval by salvagebar in site17 while I was, granted, already halfway though. In the future however, I will ask before going gung-ho, as I understand how that can go wrong.

I didn't hear anything from salvagebar, but such is the way chat is. Visibility is good!

Thank you for implementing the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: uh welp, bit too late for that, but I can leave a comment on those pages as you requested, which I believe added up to 45 articles. Getting in touch with the authors of them, probably not feasible. Ultimately the content was unchanged, I just made them more accessible, and quite frankly this is an objective upgrade. I was also given approval by salvagebar in site17 while I was, granted, already halfway though. In the future however, I will ask before going gung-ho, as I understand how that can go wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, I think you could argue this falls under "minor correction", as you're not changing the content, just fixing an obvious "error". I guess ask Zyn or someone if there are any bylaws regarding this.

I'd say this is kind of a borderline case, since while it is a fix, it's not something simple like changing the spelling of a word.

For these cases, I'm fine with experienced people who know what they're doing implementing the change, so long as the original author is notified of the change and why it was made. And also a post put in the discussion so everyone else can be kept in the loop, which has already happened here. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I did not know that was a thing lol, I'll get on that tomorrow probably, I would assume this won't be a problem since you did it without issue

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "audio" and "video" tags?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: is there a method of finding all articles that have a flash player? I know I must have seen at least a dozen. A full conversion would be ideal and I don't think it would take too much effort. Heck, I could do it myself, but I don't know how to find them all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, that seems compelling.

ETA: And is done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I think you could argue this falls under "minor correction", as you're not changing the content, just fixing an obvious "error". I guess ask Zyn or someone if there are any bylaws regarding this. It needs to be done someday; when chrome drops flash, the only way to listen to these is to download them and that's just a pain in the rear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weird, I couldn't get it to do so in In the Trenches With The Dead.

Well in that case changing the player shouldn't be a problem right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: .mp3 works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You would need to convert the files to a format that the html5player snippet supports (.ogg works), but otherwise this a trivial fix. I could do it, but I am not sure if that falls under allowed edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this site really ought to do a "purge" of sorts and reformat these audio files out of flash. Firefox doesn't even have the option of using Flash anymore, nor do mobile devices, and chrome is dropping it soon enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would Stenography (the act of hiding images and words in audio) bypass 1159's effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-510068/scp-1159#post-1611526
This may help you.Or you can download the mp3 in the "files",just at the bottom of the page(1159).Click it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All joking aside. Can you please give a text version. I don't have Adobe. I can't read this. Therefore, I can't rate it.
You got me at Dr. Androvsky's "music of information." Also, the [BLEEP]s made me jump a little.

item.title: Why all the hate?
item.content:encoded:
I personally love the idea! I thought that the audio was easy to listen to, and it felt like an actual researcher trying to more or less improvise what is usually written and reviewed. You have to remember folks, this is a researcher talking into a microphone, recording his voice, not a voice actor (in terms of in-universe. Also, not saying the voice recording itself was bad).

My only critique would be the sound balancing used in the interview and naming the test logs (i.e. all sound in my right ear would cease when the name of a test log would be said, but continue when the test explanation starts). If you are going for that effect, I would roughly do a 70% - 30% audio balance ( [Left Ear] - [Right Ear] ). It's a bit uncomfortable to me to have next to no sound in one ear, but to have 'normal' volume in the other.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Impossible on iPhones due to flash requirements on the MP3 player.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
wrapped in a titanium shell.

god dammit i'm pretty sure one of the Js is loose now.

item.title: Very Original
item.content:encoded: I like it. An object that you can't describe through visual means? Brilliant. Plus, the sound it makes is pretty nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this skip +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reality is unrealistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Audio recordings can make a good skip great, but only if you provide transcripts. For one thing, not everyone will be able listen to them. Even those who can may not wish to: for example, I personally rather hate audiobooks, because the pace of reading out loud is frustratingly glacial compared to that of silent reading. I couldn't even get through the first recording before giving up in disgust and just reading Forlorn Willow's transcript.

My opinion, in short: this might — might — be an interesting skip, especially with that "music of information" concept, but the execution destroys it. -1.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Additionally, Echo was in fact spoken to about just this exact sort of bad critique, even though it wasn't against the rules at the time (and for a long time was in fact encouraged).

After discussion with other staff, Echo spent the next few months after this (mid-to-late-2012) becoming one of the best critiquers on the site.

So there you have it.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Because we don't retroactively police posts and at the time of posting the criticism policy had not yet been developed, let alone enforced.

Ironically, I'm going to ask you to stop necroshitposting on this thread. Your comment adds nothing to the discussion.

item.title: Re: Gonna have to agree with Crayne here.
item.content:encoded: So, why exactly isn't this person banned, again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this twist on an infohazard but all the ums and whatnot really hurt my view of it.
The tone of the recordings seem a bit too conversational to me compared to the strict clinical tone I'm used to. It sounds a bit like the author didn't really rehearse the lines he wrote, and the hum in the background doesn't help. (And I did confirm it's not an issue on my end.)
I want to upvote but I need to give it some more thought before I decide either way.
Side note: I really like Androvsky's accent, fake or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me, this is the best format screw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favourite format screws on this site; it's in my opinion one of the most unique and unconventional ways an SCP can be presented. My other favourite is probably that crossword puzzle, though I like that a bit less because it forces you to solve a puzzle just to read it - it can be a problem if you are just looking for something to leisurely read.

As for difficulties for those on mobile devices, I suggest perhaps having this site allow media playback using HTML5 widgets instead of using Flash. Until that happens though, I think I have a workaround. To the author: may I have the original MP3 recordings of this SCP? I think I can upload them to YouTube, which can be viewed on most mobile devices without problems. Perhaps you can also upload MP3s of them onto something like Dropbox, where people can download and listen to on their mobile devices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the… idea of having a page made only in audio format. But other then that it's just a object that can't be described in written format.

I don't see how this is an "infohazard" though. Knowing about it won't harm you anyone. Trying to write it on the otherhand…'

No vote After hearing My pleasure and Think of it dis way I'm upvoting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In context, "Herculean" makes the most sense, since "Hergilian" isn't a word and doesn't sound like any Greek word I know. I understand the intent of "Herculean Greece" as a time period—the problem is that it's basically like saying "Imperial China" in terms of era, or "Muslim Egypt"; the author may have a specific time period or overall "style" (like architecture and general "back then" sense of time) in mind that they associate with that word/era, but the phrase (and the understanding) is painfully inadequate to be considered in any way scientific.

It's the reason analysis of artifacts uses things like "Early/High/Late Medieval era", as that roughly delineates a period of 1000 years into distinct sections of time that you can get—-"early" being the "dark ages blaargh vikings", "high" being the "crusades ho!", "late" being the "plate mail and hundred years wars and crossbows and Italians"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you're going by the transcript, i should say that "Herculean" was only my best guess for something that sounded more like "hergillian", so if you have any better transcription ideas then please correct me. (Congratulations for picking up on the one word I wasn't sure about…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Computer I'm reading this on doesn't have the proper drivers so I was faced with big empty spaces. Instant downvote.

Also an nitpick:

"Herculean Greece"

"Herculean" referring to "Hercules" which is not Greek, but a Romanization of Herakles, of which the time period in question is either entirely fictional, or imprecise—legends of Herakles and Hercules place him as far back as the mythic age, pre-Trojan War, to as far ahead as 800s BC, long after the Trojan War.

item.title: Re: Flash
item.content:encoded: If you'd like a transcript, see this comment above (which I just put a bigger font on to help people find it). If you want to listen on mobile, a thing that worked for me was clicking on "Files" at the bottom of the article page, which brings up a list of mp3 versions.

item.title: Flash
item.content:encoded: The use of Flash earned an instant downvote from me. Aside from helping support Adobe's shitty standards, it makes it impossible for mobile users to (easily) listen to this SCP. Additionally, the lack of a transcript means that if I want to actually know what this SCP is, I have to go home, boot up my computer, launch Firefox, unblock Flash player plugin, and then listen to the audio files. From what I've read on the discussion page, the SCP isn't even that good, and the recording is poor quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Thank you for joining me Dr. Androvsky."

"My pleasure"

I literally started laughing when I heard that.

item.title: Re: Hah. I just thought of this.
item.content:encoded: more like 1000 zimbabwean dollars.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had the exact same reaction. In addition, Dr. Reixis's voice acting is particularly mediocre toward the beginning of that file, which didn't help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is majorly hilarious because Dr. Androvsky is actually a Romanian guy. That is not a fake accent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thing is, I want to be able to read this on my own speed instead of hear someone else read it at their speed.

It's a good gimmick idea, but I'm not a huge fan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the idea and the execution somewhat…but the thing that really ruined it for me was the voices. They just sounded really…fake, I guess. and Dr. Androvsky's accent sounded ridiculously phony and seriously ruined it for me. I think getting someone who can do decent voice acting to rerecord the file would make it a lot better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TRANSCRIPT

As far as I know there isn't a transcription available anywhere … until now! Strictly out-of-universe, of course. (To original author: I hope you don't mind…)

+ SCP-1159
- SCP-1159

INFOHAZARD WARNING: All information in this file must be kept in auditory form.

SCP-1159 (Audio File) Note: I apologize for the informality of this recording. I was not able to use written notes. – Dr. Reixis

Item SCP-1159. Object class Safe.

Special containment procedures: SCP-1159 is to be contained in a box measuring two meters by one-half meter by one-half meter. The box is to be kept in a climate-controlled room at all times to prevent deterioration of the wood. SCP-1159, as a note, is an infohazard, and as such, all staff should be discouraged from … making any notes or other written commentary about 1159, in order to prevent data loss.

Description: SCP-1159 is an Aeolian wind harp, measuring 1.5 meters by 20 millimeters by 30 millimeters. Carbon dating suggests that this wind harp is less than two hundred years old. Markings on the underside of the harp indicate that it was manufactured in Herculean Greece, though no date is listed. Harps of this type are meant to be played by the wind with no human interaction … SCP-1159 will produce multiple harmonic tones when wind … er, or some other source of air is blown through the back of the harp, causing the strings to vibrate. An example of this harmonic tone is included in attachment 1 of this file. The anomalous nature of SCP-1159 becomes apparent when any person attempts to create any kind of written descriptions of the object. Any such attempts completely and utterly fail. Um … It's worth noting that referencing SCP-1159 itself does not produce the effect, nor do descriptions kept in a purely auditory state, which is the reason for the unorthodox, um, medium of this particular file. People who listen to SCP-1159 for some time often describe its music as “entrancing” and “beautiful” and such, but it is unclear whether or not this is an anomalous effect or whether the sounds produced by the object are simply high-quality harmonics.

Er, Addendum 1: SCP-1159 was first discovered in an auction house on [BLEEP] in [BLEEP], New York. An elderly male subject mentioned to a Foundation agent posing as customer while on standing containment patrol that he had a beautiful harp in the back of his shop. The agent asked for the price of the harp, and the subject was not able to produce one, stating that he had not had an opportunity to write it down. He did note that … that was an unusual occurrence, but he said that it was certainly just an oversight on his part. After determining the anomalous nature of the object, the agent … initiated a containment protocol, and, er, SCP-1159 was recovered.

Ending recording.

SCP-1159 (Addendum 1159-A): Test 1.

Addendum 1159 tack A: Attempts to write down descriptions of SCP-1159 have not been met with success. In this first tests, subjects were given various mediums with which to capture a description of 1159, including pens, pencils, cameras, typewriters, and computers. Upon initiation of the tests, er, no subjects were able to complete the task. Pencils broke, mostly tips; one pencil was found to have no lead in it … um, pens began leaking all over the paper, um, two of the pens had their tips rupture … Er, the cameras were also unsuccessful: the digital camera suffered corrupted files, and, er, one suffered a short circuit; the analog cameras found that all of their film had been overexposed. The computers all suffered fatal malfunctions, and, er, the typewriter suffered a mechanical malfunction including a broken gear, and, er, several broken keys.

SCP-1159 (Addendum 1159-B): Test 2.

Addendum 1159 tack B: Subject was given a lead pencil created by Foundation engineers with the purpose of making a pencil able to write under extreme conditions. Pencil was created with lead alloy tip to prevent breaking, and was wrapped in a titanium shell. On initiation of the test, subject suffered a fatal heart attack. Further testing has been suspended until further notice.

End recording.

SCP-1159 (Addendum 1159-C): Interview with Dr. Androvsky following events of Test 2.

This is Dr. Buchanan; audio log of interview with Dr. Androvsky. Dr. Androvsky is a Foundation researcher stationed at Site [BLEEP] and will give his opinion on SCP-1159.

Dr. Buchanan: Thank you for joining me, Dr. Androvsky.

Dr. Androvsky: My pleasure.

Dr. Buchanan: You've had some time to study SCP-1159 and its anomalous effects; have you formed any opinions as to the nature of the harp?

Dr. Androvsky: I have. I believe that the reason the object behave the way that it does is a form of self … preservation.

Dr. Buchanan: How so?

Dr. Androvsky: … Think of it this way: Aeolian wind harps do not need humans to play them. The only interaction the human has with the harp is by listening to the music. If you take away the audience, the harp has nothing. You can describe a harp with words, certainly, but in anomalous effect of the harp forces anyone who wishes to learn about the harp to learn about it auditorily. Even if the person … never hears the sound of the harp itself, the simple fact that information must disperse through sound is music on itself – the music of information.

Dr. Buchanan: Thank you once again, Dr. Androvsky.

Dr. Androvsky: My pleasure, again.

- SCP-1159

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot experience this due to technical speaker problems and annoying people in the room with me. Is there a transcribed version somewhere that I can read, Out Of Universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was cool, anyway. The tone matched what you'd expect in an article, only verbally dictated. I immediately thought of Doom 3's PDA audiologs. The idea and format is original, impressively so. We need a few more of these, I don't care what anyone else says. +1 from me.

item.title: Re: Don't feel it
item.content:encoded:
the idea is sound,

dohoho

item.title: Don't feel it
item.content:encoded: I gotta agree with a lot of the others on this one, the idea is sound, but the execution isn't very good, and the actual depth to the piece isn't exceptionally interesting. It could do with some better writing, and a completely re-done, well rehearsed read. Unless that happens, I gotta give it a -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know exactly the square root of nothing about audio tech, but if there is anyway to overcome this limitation please do so. You would have to re-do the description and mention that it adds itself to all audio regarding itself, but I guess you would have to re-record anyway to add the music? Like I said, I know nothing about audio.

Right now, you have a okay-to-poor item and a novel presentation, which averages out to…average. Integrating the item more thoroughly into the format would overcome a lot of the negativity people have about this just being a gimick.

Splitting the containment and description into different audio files might help with the "wall o' text" like reaction some people are having, where they object to sitting through the entire play back. Personally, I think they just don't like the unexpected change to the routine they are used to.

Way to be original, don't let any negativity you hear deter you from trying other experiments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hurr dee frickin' durr. I don't care how much money you foolishly blew on a recording setup, if you don't know how to master audio, you're fucked no matter how good your mic is. Cut out the noise frequencies, whether it's your computer or just background mess, filter or smooth that out. Also, EQ your own voice so it doesn't sound so muddy. Don't be afraid to put some atmosphere around yourself either. [In other words, fill in the empty space between your words with something pleasant.]

Your voice itself is not putting on a good performance. Your tone is not serious, and your reading style is, um, unprofessional. Write your shit down, and read it back. The police are, for the most part, bumbling idiots (no offense). The police also do not face mass casualties and end-of-the-world scenarios if they fuck up or someone misunderstands them.

Also, the dry dial tone expungement during the interview scared the shit out of me. Fix that to something else or at least keep it from jumping the fuck out of everything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You actually lose audio quality due to loss of dynamic range through high-ratio compression or "limiting". That is what the 'Loudness war' is about, not clipping. That's just bad mastering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's pretty much what I was afraid of. I don't know if it'd be possible to have the voice through, say, all channels and only have the sound of the harp through the left speakers or whatever. I don't know enough about mixing and audio formats.

Perhaps the volume can adjust during the playback depending on its "mood" to the tape? (To carry on Dr E Cay's suggestion from below. I think it would be okay to occasionally drown out the spoken part, but it'd be easy to make it really annoying.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 to this comment, the sound of the instrument just barely audible in the background would make it more interesting to listen to, if just to find out where the noise was coming from. Perhaps you could even make it get louder at times and quieter during others, just some weird audio anomaly. Keep working on it, you can figure this out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not… sure that's the standard by which Classes are defined.

The designation "Keter" is assigned to subjects that both (a) display vigorous, active hostility to human life, civilization, and/or spacetime, and (b) are capable of causing significant destruction in the event of a containment breach.

An SCP object is classified as Euclid when its behavior cannot be unerringly predicted, either because the item is sentient, it behaves outside of current scientific knowledge, or its nature is simply poorly understood at present.

Yes, I know Hurtz go to it before I did, but I felt that it needed to be said.
